prompt:
Trade-billing incident, steel service center. Three separate complaints landed on the desk this week, all against the invoicing service in this directory, and month-end close is stuck behind them. I've hand-checked every number below against the paper agreements with a calculator, so trust the arithmetic; I have no idea where in the code any of this lives.

1) Edited invoices keep billing the OLD amount. Inside sales opens a draft with 12 sticks of 2x1/4 flat bar at $45.50 — portal shows $546.00, correct. Customer calls back, we bump the line to 20 sticks, portal still says $546.00. Re-print, still $546.00. Bounce the service and the same draft prints $910.00 like nothing was ever wrong. Adding a whole new line does the same thing — the total acts frozen at whatever it was the first time anyone looked at it.

2) Accounts on early-settlement terms (2/10 net 30) are short-paying us by exactly one cent, and their AP clerks are right. Example from the Bellhaven remittance: merchandise $1,038.25, 2% early-settlement discount is $20.765 and every trade agreement we print says halves round UP, so the discount is $20.77 and the amount due is $1,017.48. Our statement demands $1,017.49. Plain trade-discount accounts (7.5% etc.) tie out to the penny every time, so it's specifically the early-settlement figure. Finance flagged 41 invoices like this in March alone.

3) Month-end settlement crashes outright on some invoices: java.util.ConcurrentModificationException, and the batch dies. Pattern we found: it's exactly the invoices whose freight charge ran past the 5%-of-merchandise contract cap — the ones where we owe the customer a freight-cap credit line. Invoices with freight under the cap settle clean. Until this is fixed we cannot close March.

Repro: `java TestMain.java` in this directory fails on exactly these behaviors (and pins the healthy paths that must not regress). Could be one root cause or three — please find out, fix the service properly, and don't touch TestMain.java; the expectations in it are exactly what the trade agreements say.
